content:
# Leviticus Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60v–61r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_007_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_007_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 91–92 (pa_0091–pa_0092)

---

**1** "And this is the instruction of the guilt offering; it is most holy.

**2** In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, they shall slaughter the guilt offering, and its blood shall be thrown against the altar on all sides.

**3** And all its fat he shall offer from it: the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails,

**4** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.

**5** And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD; it is a guilt offering.

**6** Every male among the priests may eat of it. It shall be eaten in a holy place; it is most holy.

**7** The guilt offering is like the sin offering; there is one instruction for them. The priest who makes atonement with it shall have it.

**8** And the priest who offers any man's burnt offering, that priest shall have for himself the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered.

**9** And every grain offering baked in an oven and every grain offering prepared in a pan or on a griddle shall belong to the priest who offers it.

**10** And every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall belong to all the sons of Aaron, one as much as another.

**11** "And this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings that one may offer to the LORD.

**12** If he offers it as a thanksgiving, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil.

**13** With the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offerings he shall bring his offering with loaves of leavened bread.

**14** And from it he shall offer one loaf from each offering, as a contribution to the LORD; it shall belong to the priest who throws the blood of the peace offerings.

**15** And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offerings shall be eaten on the day of his offering. He shall not leave any of it until the morning.

**16** But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day that he offers his sacrifice, and on the next day what remains of it shall be eaten.

**17** But what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned up with fire.

**18** If any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings is eaten on the third day, he who offers it shall not be accepted, and it shall not be credited to him. It shall be an abomination, and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity.

**19** Flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten. It shall be burned up with fire. As for the flesh, everyone who is clean may eat the flesh.

**20** But the person who eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belong to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him, that person shall be cut off from his people.

**21** And if anyone touches an unclean thing — whether the uncleanness of man or an unclean beast or any unclean detestable creature — and then eats some of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belong to the LORD, that person shall be cut off from his people."

**22** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**23** "Speak to the people of Israel, saying: You shall eat no fat, of ox or sheep or goat.

**24** The fat of an animal that dies of itself and the fat of one that is torn by beasts may be put to any other use, but you shall by no means eat it.

**25** For every person who eats of the fat from an animal of which a fire offering may be made to the LORD shall be cut off from his people.

**26** Moreover, you shall eat no blood whatever, whether of bird or of animal, in any of your dwelling places.

**27** Any person who eats any blood, that person shall be cut off from his people."

**28** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**29** "Speak to the people of Israel, saying: Whoever offers the sacrifice of his peace offerings to the LORD shall bring his offering to the LORD from the sacrifice of his peace offerings.

**30** His own hands shall bring the LORD's fire offerings. He shall bring the fat with the breast, so that the breast may be waved as a wave offering before the LORD.

**31** The priest shall burn the fat on the altar, but the breast shall be for Aaron and his sons.

**32** And the right thigh you shall give to the priest as a contribution from the sacrifice of your peace offerings.

**33** Whoever among the sons of Aaron offers the blood of the peace offerings and the fat shall have the right thigh for a portion.

**34** For the breast that is waved and the thigh that is contributed I have taken from the people of Israel, from the sacrifices of their peace offerings, and have given them to Aaron the priest and to his sons as a perpetual statute from the people of Israel.

**35** This is the anointed portion of Aaron and of his sons from the LORD's fire offerings, from the day they were presented to serve as priests for the LORD,

**36** which the LORD commanded to be given them, from the day that he anointed them, from the people of Israel. It is a perpetual statute throughout their generations.

**37** This is the instruction of the burnt offering, of the grain offering, of the sin offering, of the guilt offering, of the ordination offering, and of the sacrifice of peace offerings,

**38** which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day that he commanded the people of Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60v–61r.** Both manuscript pages were examined directly. Folio 60v carries the opening of Leviticus 7 — beginning with the instruction of the guilt offering (vv. 1–10) — alongside the closing material of chapter 6. The columnar layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly visible, written in the characteristic square Tiberian script with Masoretic pointing. The chapter moves without dramatic pause from chapter 6's final verse into the guilt offering instruction section, reflecting the broader priestly torah-block that spans chapters 6–7 as a unified instruction manual for the priests.

Folio 61r carries the remainder of chapter 7, from the peace offering instructions (vv. 11ff.) through the concluding colophon (vv. 37–38). The three-column layout of the folio is legible throughout. The colophon at vv. 37–38 is visually distinctive — it summarizes the entire sacrificial instruction block of chapters 1–7 in a single periodic sentence, listing all six offering types: olah (burnt offering), minchah (grain offering), chattat (sin offering), asham (guilt offering), milu'im (ordination offerings), and shelamim (peace offerings). The manuscript shows this list clearly in folio 61r.

The parashah structure within chapter 7 is notable. The peace offering subsection (vv. 11–21) is marked by the introductory formula זֹאת תּוֹרַת זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים (zot torat zevach ha-shelamim, "this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings"), continuing the chapter 6 series of zot torat formulae. A new speech unit begins at v. 22 with the standard vayyedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor formula, introducing the blood and fat prohibition for all Israel (vv. 22–27). Another new speech unit begins at v. 28 with the same formula, introducing the wave offering and contribution procedures (vv. 28–36). The final summary colophon (vv. 37–38) closes the entire priestly instruction block of Lev 1–7.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 91–92 (LXX, pa_0091–pa_0092).** Both Vaticanus pages were examined directly. Page 91 (pa_0091) carries the opening portion of the Leviticus 7 LXX text, continuing from the end of the chapter 6 material. The chapter heading CAP. XXXV is visible in the margin of page 92 (pa_0092), indicating the LXX's own chapter enumeration. The three-column uncial hand is readable throughout. Page 92 completes the chapter through the summary colophon, with the chapter number marker visible.

The LXX text of Leviticus 7 follows the MT structure closely, with no major textual dislocations. Several notable LXX readings diverge from the MT in terminology and phrasing, particularly in the technical cultic vocabulary for the peace offering subcategories and the wave/contribution offering procedure. These are documented in the variants table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"This is the instruction of the guilt offering; it is most holy" (v. 1).** Hebrew זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָאָשָׁם קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא (zot torat ha-asham qodesh qodashim hu — "this is the instruction of the guilt offering; most holy it is"). The verse opens chapter 7 with the formula established in chapter 6 (zot torat). The asham (guilt offering) receives its own instruction section, parallel to the chattat's section at 6:24–30. The declaration qodesh qodashim hu ("it is most holy") immediately assigns the guilt offering to the supreme sacral category, placing it alongside the sin offering as a most-holy-things offering. "It is most holy" renders the predicate nominative clause precisely. The LXX renders ἅγιον τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν ("it is holy of holies"), in direct agreement.

2. **"Its blood shall be thrown against the altar on all sides" (v. 2).** Hebrew וְאֶת-דָּמוֹ יִזְרֹק עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב (ve-et-damo yizroq al-ha-mizbeach saviv — "and its blood he shall throw against the altar all around"). The verb זָרַק (zaraq, "to throw, to dash, to sprinkle with force") describes the blood-throwing procedure at the altar — not the delicate hyssop-sprinkling of the sanctuary procedures but the forceful tossing of blood from a basin against the sides of the altar. "Thrown against ... on all sides" renders zaraq ... saviv accurately. The same procedure was prescribed for the burnt offering (Lev 1:5) and the peace offering (Lev 3:2). The LXX renders προσχεεῖ ... κύκλῳ ("shall pour against ... around"), using προσχέω ("to pour toward/against") for zaraq — slightly less forceful than the MT's "throwing" but capturing the distribution idea via κύκλῳ ("all around / in a circle").

3. **"The fat tail" (v. 3).** Hebrew הָאַלְיָה (ha-alyah — "the fat tail"). The alyah is the distinctive broad, fatty tail of the Near Eastern fat-tailed sheep (Ovis aries), a specific organ removed and burned along with the chelev (interior fat) as a fire offering. It appears also in Lev 3:9 in the peace offering instructions. "Fat tail" (ESV, NASB) renders ha-alyah as precisely as possible in English, since the specific anatomy has no single English noun equivalent. The LXX renders τὸν λοβόν ("the lobe") at this point — possibly a misreading or conflation with the liver's lobe, or a simplified rendering. The MT's alyah is clearly the fat tail; the LXX's λοβόν is more ambiguous. This translation follows the MT.

4. **"The sacrifice of peace offerings" (vv. 11–18, passim).** Hebrew זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים (zevach ha-shelamim — "the sacrifice of the peace offerings"). The term zevach (sacrifice, a slaughtered offering involving shared consumption) is here combined with shelamim (peace/wholeness offerings) to form the full technical designation of this category. Chapter 7 consistently uses zevach ha-shelamim throughout the peace offering instruction section, in contrast to chapter 3's use of just qorban shelamim ("an offering of peace offerings"). "Sacrifice of peace offerings" renders zevach ha-shelamim accurately, preserving both elements. The LXX renders θυσία σωτηρίου ("sacrifice of salvation/peace") or θυσίαν σωτηρίου, using σωτήριον ("salvation offering") for shelamim — the standard LXX rendering of shelamim throughout Leviticus.

5. **"Thanksgiving" / todah (v. 12).** Hebrew תּוֹדָה (todah — "thanksgiving, confession, praise"). The todah is the highest-grade subcategory of the peace offering, offered in response to an experience of divine deliverance or blessing — illness survived, danger escaped, prayer answered. Rabbinic tradition (notably in b. Pesachim 79b, citing this very legislation) holds that the todah will be the only offering that continues in the messianic age. "Thanksgiving" is the consistent rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV) and captures the gratitude-response character of the todah accurately. The LXX renders αἰνέσεως ("of praise"), using αἴνεσις — praise/laudation — rather than the explicit "thanksgiving" word εὐχαριστία, but the LXX's αἴνεσις and the MT's todah both convey the idea of verbal/sacrificial acknowledgment of divine goodness. This translation uses "thanksgiving" to align with the MT's specific term.

6. **"Unleavened loaves mixed with oil / unleavened wafers smeared with oil / loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil" (v. 12).** Hebrew חַלּוֹת מַצּוֹת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשֶּׁמֶן וּרְקִיקֵי מַצּוֹת מְשֻׁחִים בַּשֶּׁמֶן וְסֹלֶת מֻרְבֶּכֶת חַלֹּת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשֶּׁמֶן (challot matsot belulot ba-shemen u-reqiqei matsot meshuchim ba-shemen ve-solet murbbekhet challot belulot ba-shemen). Three types of bread offerings accompany the thanksgiving peace offering: (a) challot matsot belulot — unleavened loaves (flat breads) mixed with oil; (b) reqiqei matsot meshuchim — unleavened wafers thinly spread with oil (from the root meshach, "to smear, to anoint, to spread"); (c) solet murbbekhet challot belulot — loaves of fine flour that are murbbekhet (well-mixed/kneaded, the same hapax as in 6:21) and belulot (mixed with oil). The three-fold variety underscores the celebratory abundance of the todah. "Unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil" renders each type distinctly. The LXX renders the three categories with ἄρτους ἀζύμους ἀναπεποιημένους ἐν ἐλαίῳ ("unleavened breads kneaded with oil"), λαγάνοις ἀζύμοις κεχρισμένοις ἐν ἐλαίῳ ("unleavened flat cakes anointed with oil"), and σεμιδάλεως πεφυραμένης ἐν ἐλαίῳ ("fine flour kneaded with oil"), in close three-part agreement with the MT categories.

7. **"Loaves of leavened bread" (v. 13).** Hebrew עַל-חַלֹּת לֶחֶם חָמֵץ (al-challot lechem chamets — "upon loaves of leavened bread"). The thanksgiving peace offering is unique among the offerings in requiring that leavened bread (chamets) be brought alongside the three unleavened bread types. This is the only sacrificial context in Leviticus where leavened bread is explicitly mandated. The leavened loaves are not burned on the altar — they are given to the priest as part of the contribution (v. 14) and form part of the communal meal. The contrast between the unleavened breads offered on the altar and the leavened bread given for the meal is significant: leaven represents the fullness of ordinary domestic life that is incorporated into the thanksgiving celebration. "Loaves of leavened bread" renders challot lechem chamets accurately. The LXX renders ἄρτους ζυμωτούς ("leavened breads"), in close agreement.

8. **"A contribution to the LORD" / terumah (v. 14).** Hebrew תְּרוּמָה לַיהוָה (terumah la-YHWH — "a contribution/elevation to the LORD"). The terumah is an offering that is "lifted" or "raised" before the LORD — the root rum (רוּם) means to be high, to raise up. It designates a portion set apart from a larger quantity and given to the LORD (and thereby to the priest). The terumah in v. 14 is the one leavened loaf from each basket of thanksgiving bread that is designated for the officiating priest. "Contribution" (ESV, NASB: "offering"; NJPS: "gift") renders terumah as a formal designation. The term will recur in the wave offering legislation (v. 32: "contribution," terumah, for the right thigh). The LXX renders ἀφαίρεμα ("what is taken off / separated portion"), using ἀφαίρεμα — a portion separated from the whole — for terumah consistently throughout Leviticus.

9. **"Abomination" / piggul (v. 18).** Hebrew פִּגּוּל (piggul — "abomination, foul thing, rejected thing"). The word piggul occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible: here, Lev 19:7, and Ezek 4:14. It designates specifically sacrificial flesh that has been rendered ritually invalid — "foul" or "abominable" — through improper intention during the offering procedure, particularly the intention to eat the sacrifice beyond its permissible time limit (the so-called piggul intention, which invalidates the entire offering even if it has not yet been eaten). The rendering here of piggul as "abomination" (NASB: "an offensive thing"; ESV: "tainted") follows the older tradition of rendering piggul with a strong disgust-word. The LXX renders εἰδωλόθυτον ("idol-sacrifice / food offered to idols"), which is a dramatic interpretive rendering — it does not mean the MT's piggul is about idolatry, but the LXX evidently associated invalid/rejected sacrifice with the category of forbidden pagan food. This translation follows the MT with "abomination," noting the LXX divergence.

10. **"Shall bear his iniquity" (v. 18).** Hebrew עֲוֹנוֹ יִשָּׂא (avono yissa — "his iniquity he shall bear"). The idiom nasa' avon ("to bear iniquity") is the Levitical formula for personal culpability that remains upon the offender. The person who eats piggul-flesh bears his own iniquity — divine penalty is retained. This phrase complements the karet formula ("shall be cut off") of vv. 20–21 and 25: in some cases the penalty is stated as karet (divine excision), in v. 18 as nasa' avon (bearing iniquity without atonement). "Shall bear his iniquity" renders avono yissa literally and idiomatically. The LXX renders λήμψεται τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ("shall take/receive the sin"), using λαμβάνω ("to receive, to take") for nasa' — a slightly different metaphor (receiving/taking rather than carrying), but semantically close.

11. **"Shall be cut off" / karet (vv. 20, 21, 25, 27).** Hebrew וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא (ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi — "and that person/soul shall be cut off"). The karet penalty — "cutting off" from the people — is the most severe human penalty mentioned in Leviticus, short of capital execution. The precise meaning of karet is debated: is it divine excision (God removes the person from the community and/or from eternal life), judicial execution, premature death, or childlessness? The most careful scholarly consensus is that karet is a divine penalty — God himself acts to "cut off" the offender — as distinct from human judicial execution. The penalty is invoked throughout chapter 7 for: eating peace offerings while unclean (v. 20), eating peace offerings after touching an unclean thing (v. 21), eating chelev (fat) of an animal fit for fire offering (v. 25), and eating blood (v. 27). "Shall be cut off from his people" (ESV, NASB, KJV) renders ve-nikhretah ... me-amehah accurately. The LXX renders ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ("shall be utterly destroyed"), using a strong verb for total destruction — somewhat more violent than "cutting off," but conveying the finality of the penalty. This translation follows the MT's "cut off."

12. **"Fat" / chelev (vv. 23–25).** Hebrew חֵלֶב (chelev — "fat, suet"). Chelev designates the specific internal fat deposits that are reserved exclusively for the LORD in the sacrificial system: the fat covering the entrails, the kidney fat, and the liver lobe fat (as described in the specific offering instructions of chapters 3–7). The prohibition in vv. 23–25 extends the cultic chelev-reservation into a universal dietary prohibition: no Israelite may eat chelev of ox, sheep, or goat anywhere. The fat of non-sacrificial animals that die naturally or are torn by beasts (which cannot be sacrificed) may still be used for other purposes (lamp oil, tanning, etc.) but may not be eaten. "Fat" renders chelev consistently. The LXX renders στέαρ ("fat, suet"), the standard Greek term for animal fat used in sacrificial contexts — in close agreement.

13. **"Wave offering" / tenufah (v. 30).** Hebrew תְּנוּפָה (tenufah — "wave offering, waving"). The tenufah is a ritual gesture performed before the LORD: the offerer brings the breast of the peace offering on his own hands (v. 30), and the priest waves it — moves it in a back-and-forth or up-and-down motion before the altar — as a presentation gesture acknowledging the LORD's ownership before it is designated for the priests. "Wave offering" (ESV, NASB, KJV) renders tenufah consistently. The LXX renders ἐπίθεμα ("that which is placed upon / that which is added"), a significantly different rendering that does not capture the waving motion — the LXX term implies placement or setting before rather than a waving gesture. The MT's specific waving procedure is preserved in this translation.

14. **"Contribution" / terumah for the thigh (v. 32).** Hebrew תְּרוּמָה (terumah — "contribution, elevation offering"). The right thigh (shōq ha-yamin) is designated as a terumah ("contribution") to the priest, just as the leavened loaf in v. 14 was a terumah. The distinction between the waved breast (tenufah, v. 30) and the contributed thigh (terumah, v. 32) is maintained in the MT: the breast is waved (tenufah, given to all the priests collectively — to Aaron and his sons, v. 31), while the right thigh is a terumah designated for the specific officiating priest (v. 33: "whoever ... offers the blood ... shall have the right thigh for a portion"). "Contribution" renders terumah consistently with v. 14. The LXX renders ἀφαίρεμα ("separated portion") for terumah in v. 32, consistent with its rendering at v. 14.

15. **"The anointed portion of Aaron and of his sons" (v. 35).** Hebrew זֹאת מִשְׁחַת אַהֲרֹן וּמִשְׁחַת בָּנָיו (zot mishchat Aharon u-mishchat banav — "this is the anointed portion / the anointing-portion of Aaron and of his sons"). The word מִשְׁחָה (mishchah) here is unusual — it comes from the root משח (mashach, "to anoint") and in this context designates the priestly portion as the "anointing portion" or "anointed share" — the share that belongs to the anointed priests. Some translations render mishchah as "allotted portion" (NJPS) or "portion" (ESV: "this is the anointed portion"). "Anointed portion" preserves the connection to the act of anointing that established the priestly office, rendering the derivation visible. The LXX renders τὸ χρῖσμα Ααρων καὶ τὸ χρῖσμα τῶν υἱῶν αὐτοῦ ("the anointing / the chrism of Aaron and the chrism of his sons"), using χρῖσμα — the same word used for the anointing oil — in direct agreement with the MT's derivation from mashach.

16. **"Ordination offering" / milu'im (v. 37).** Hebrew הַמִּלֻּאִים (ha-milu'im — "the ordination offerings / the fillings"). The milu'im (from male', "to fill") is the offering of the ordination/installation ceremony for Aaron and his sons described in Leviticus 8. Its appearance in the closing colophon of v. 37 is forward-looking: the colophon summarizes the torot of Lev 1–7 but anticipates the coming milu'im ceremony. "Ordination offering" (ESV, NASB) renders milu'im accurately in the cultic context. The LXX renders τελειώσεων ("of completions / of perfections / of ordinations"), using τελείωσις — completion or perfection — which is the LXX's consistent rendering of milu'im. This is theologically rich: the LXX terminology suggests the ordination offerings as "completions" or "perfections" of the sacrificial system, inaugurating the priesthood.

17. **"On Mount Sinai / in the wilderness of Sinai" (v. 38).** Hebrew בְּמִדְבַּר סִינַי (be-midbar Sinai — "in the wilderness of Sinai"). The colophon anchors the entire sacrificial instruction of Lev 1–7 at Sinai — the site of the covenant. The reference to "Mount Sinai" in v. 38a (al-har Sinai — "on Mount Sinai") and "the wilderness of Sinai" in v. 38b (be-midbar Sinai) creates a twofold geographical anchoring: the revelation was given on the mountain but to a people dwelling in the wilderness below. This colophon is significant because Leviticus 1:1 begins "the LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting" — suggesting the revelation is localized at the tent. The colophon of 7:38 reaches back further, affirming that the entire Sinai revelation underlies what is now enacted at the tent. The LXX agrees with the MT's dual geographical reference throughout.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 91–92, pa_0091–pa_0092) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | qodesh qodashim hu ("most holy it is") | ἅγιον τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν ("it is holy of holies") | LXX renders the Hebrew superlative construct chain as a genitive of specification, as consistently throughout Leviticus. Full structural agreement. |
| 7:2 | yizroq ... saviv ("shall throw ... all around") | προσχεεῖ ... κύκλῳ ("shall pour against ... around") | LXX uses προσχέω ("to pour toward/against") for zaraq ("to throw/dash"), slightly softening the force. MT implies a more vigorous throwing gesture. |
| 7:3 | ha-alyah ("the fat tail") | τὸν λοβόν ("the lobe") | Notable divergence. MT's alyah is the fat tail of the sheep. LXX renders τὸν λοβόν ("the lobe"), which is the term elsewhere used for the liver's lobe (τὸν λοβὸν τοῦ ἥπατος). This may reflect a scribal confusion or a different reading tradition. The MT is clear: alyah is the fat tail. |
| 7:11 | zot torat zevach ha-shelamim ("this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς θυσίας τοῦ σωτηρίου ("this is the law of the sacrifice of the salvation offering") | LXX renders shelamim consistently as σωτήριον ("salvation/peace offering"). Standard LXX rendering throughout. |
| 7:12 | todah ("thanksgiving") | αἰνέσεως ("of praise") | LXX renders todah with αἴνεσις ("praise, laudation") rather than a word for thanksgiving per se. The semantic range overlaps but the LXX term emphasizes vocal/liturgical praise more than the MT's term, which encompasses gratitude-response broadly. |
| 7:12 | murbbekhet ("well-mixed/kneaded") | πεφυραμένης ("kneaded") | LXX renders the hapax murbbekhet consistently with its rendering at Lev 6:21 (πεφυραμένη), confirming the kneading/mixing interpretation. |
| 7:13 | challot lechem chamets ("loaves of leavened bread") | ἄρτους ζυμωτούς ("leavened breads") | LXX renders challot lechem chamets simply as "leavened breads," without specifying challot (loaves/round cakes). The LXX also — in some manuscripts — places the leavened loaves alongside the other breads rather than as a separate clause. Vaticanus here follows the standard LXX order closely matching MT. |
| 7:14 | terumah ("contribution / elevation offering") | ἀφαίρεμα ("separated portion") | LXX renders terumah consistently as ἀφαίρεμα ("that which is taken off/separated") throughout Leviticus. The MT's lifting/elevation sense (rum root) is replaced by the separation sense. Both describe a portion designated for the priest but from different conceptual frameworks. |
| 7:18 | piggul ("abomination / foul thing") | εἰδωλόθυτον ("idol-sacrifice / food offered to idols") | Significant LXX divergence. The MT's piggul is a technical term for sacrificial invalidity through improper intention (especially eating past the time limit). The LXX's εἰδωλόθυτον ("food sacrificed to idols") is an interpretive translation that shifts the category from ritual invalidity to pagan contamination. This suggests the LXX translator may have understood piggul through the lens of Gentile forbidden food rather than intra-Israelite ritual violation. This translation follows the MT. |
| 7:20 | ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi ("that person shall be cut off") | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ("that soul shall be utterly destroyed") | LXX renders karet with ἐξολεθρεύω ("to destroy utterly/completely"), a stronger verb than "to cut off." The LXX consistently uses this rendering for karet throughout Leviticus, intensifying the penalty's finality. |
| 7:21 | sheqets ("unclean detestable creature") | ἀκαθαρσίᾳ ἀνθρώπου ἢ τετραπόδου ἀκαθάρτου ἢ παντὸς βδελύγματος ἀκαθάρτου ("uncleanness of man or unclean four-footed animal or any unclean abomination") | LXX expands slightly in this verse, providing more explicit categories. The MT's kol-sheqets ("any unclean detestable creature") is rendered as "any unclean abomination" — the LXX adds four-footed beast (τετράποδον) making explicit what the MT implies. |
| 7:23 | chelev shor ve-chesev ve-ez ("the fat of ox or sheep or goat") | στέαρ μόσχου καὶ προβάτου καὶ αἰγός ("fat of calf and sheep and goat") | LXX renders shor ("ox, bull") as μόσχος ("calf"), its standard LXX rendering. The MT's shor is a general bovine; the LXX specifies calf. Minor divergence. |
| 7:24 | nevela ("animal that dies of itself") | θνησιμαῖα ("carrion / animal that died naturally") | LXX renders nevela with θνησιμαῖα ("things that died [of themselves]"), in close agreement. |
| 7:24 | terefah ("one that is torn by beasts") | θηριάλωτα ("things seized by wild animals") | LXX renders terefah with θηριάλωτα ("seized by wild beasts"), in close agreement. |
| 7:30 | tenufah ("wave offering") | ἐπίθεμα ("what is placed upon / addition") | Notable divergence. The MT's tenufah is the waving gesture before the LORD. The LXX's ἐπίθεμα ("that which is laid upon / placed upon") suggests presentation by placement rather than waving. This may reflect a different ritual understanding or a LXX tradition that did not emphasize the waving motion. The MT's waving procedure is well-attested elsewhere (Lev 8:27; 23:11, etc.). |
| 7:32 | terumah ("contribution") | ἀφαίρεμα ("separated portion") | Consistent LXX rendering of terumah throughout; see v. 14 note. |
| 7:35 | mishchat Aharon u-mishchat banav ("the anointed portion of Aaron and of his sons") | τὸ χρῖσμα Ααρων καὶ τὸ χρῖσμα τῶν υἱῶν αὐτοῦ ("the chrism/anointing of Aaron and the chrism of his sons") | LXX renders mishchah with χρῖσμα (the anointing / the chrism), directly paralleling the MT's derivation from the anointing act. Full structural agreement. |
| 7:37 | ha-milu'im ("the ordination offerings") | τελειώσεων ("of ordinations / of completions / of perfections") | LXX renders milu'im consistently as τελείωσις throughout Leviticus (also Lev 8). The LXX term suggests "completion" or "perfection," carrying a slightly different theological valence from the MT's "fillings" (from male', "to fill"). |
| 7:37 | zot ha-torah la-olah ve-la-minchah ve-la-chattat ve-la-asham ve-la-milu'im ve-li-zevach ha-shelamim ("this is the instruction of the burnt offering and of the grain offering and of the sin offering and of the guilt offering and of the ordination offerings and of the sacrifice of peace offerings") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς ὁλοκαυτώσεως καὶ θυσίας καὶ τῆς περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας καὶ τῆς πλημμελείας καὶ τῆς τελειώσεως καὶ τῆς θυσίας τοῦ σωτηρίου ("this is the law of the whole burnt offering and the sacrifice and the [offering] concerning sin and the [offering for] transgression/error and the ordination and the sacrifice of the salvation/peace offering") | LXX renders the six-offering summary faithfully. Notable: LXX renders asham with πλημμελεία ("transgression/failure/error") rather than any term directly cognate with "guilt." This reflects the LXX's understanding of the asham as an offering for inadvertent error or transgression, which is consistent with the asham's function in Lev 5–7. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 7)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| תּוֹדָה (todah) | thanksgiving | Peace offering subcategory; gratitude-response to divine deliverance; LXX: αἴνεσις ("praise") |
| נֶדֶר (neder) | vow | Peace offering subcategory; offering in fulfillment of a vow; LXX: εὐχή |
| נְדָבָה (nedavah) | freewill offering | Peace offering subcategory; spontaneous, unconditioned gift; LXX: αἵρεσις ("free choice") |
| תְּנוּפָה (tenufah) | wave offering | Ritual gesture of presentation before the LORD; LXX: ἐπίθεμα (diverges from waving concept) |
| תְּרוּמָה (terumah) | contribution | Portion lifted/raised and designated for the priest; LXX: ἀφαίρεמα |
| פִּגּוּל (piggul) | abomination | Invalid sacrifice rendered foul by improper intention; LXX: εἰδωλόθυτον (diverges significantly) |
| כָּרֵת (karet) | cut off | Divine excision penalty; "shall be cut off from his people"; LXX: ἐξολεθρευθήσεται |
| אַלְיָה (alyah) | fat tail | The broad fat tail of the Near Eastern sheep; LXX: τὸν λοβόν (diverges — "lobe") |
| מִשְׁחָה (mishchah) | anointed portion | The priests' share, derived from the anointing; LXX: χρῖσμα |
| מִלֻּאִים (milu'im) | ordination offering | The installation/ordination sacrifice; from male' ("to fill"); LXX: τελείωσις ("completion/perfection") |
| שֹׁק (shoq) | thigh | The leg-portion of the sacrifice; specifically the right thigh as priestly contribution |
| חֵלֶב (chelev) | fat | The reserved interior fat portions; LXX: στέαρ |
| דָּם (dam) | blood | Universal prohibition for Israel; LXX: αἷμα |
| תּוֹרָה (torah, cultic) | instruction | Procedural directive; established from Lev 6; LXX: νόμος |
| זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים (zevach ha-shelamim) | sacrifice of peace offerings | Full designation in Lev 7; LXX: θυσία σωτηρίου |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy | Supreme sacral category; established from Lev 6; LXX: ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων |
| עָוֹן (avon) | iniquity | The guilt/culpability that remains upon the offender; nasa' avon = "bear iniquity" |
| נְבֵלָה (nevela) | animal that dies of itself | Carcass of natural death; LXX: θνησιμαῖα |
| טְרֵפָה (terefah) | one that is torn by beasts | Animal killed by predators; LXX: θηριάλωτα |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2 |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4; LXX: περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας |
| אָשָׁם (asham) | guilt offering | Established from Lev 5; LXX: πλημμελεία in colophon |
| שְׁלָמִים (shelamim) | peace offerings | Established from Lev 3; LXX: σωτήριον |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |

— LOGOS
